---
title: "4.10 Implementing ArrayList Algorithms"
subtitle: "Data Collections"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Topic Intro

### ArrayList algorithms must respect shifting

The same collection patterns appear again:

::: {.tight-list}
- add and remove elements
- min, max, sum, average
- count properties
- find duplicates
- rotate and reverse
:::

The main extra risk is that `ArrayList` indices shift after insertions and removals.

## Remove in a Loop

### Traverse backward when removing

```java
for (int i = list.size() - 1; i >= 0; i--)
{
    if (list.get(i) < 0)
    {
        list.remove(i);
    }
}
```

Backward traversal avoids skipping elements after removal.

## Sum and Average

### Accumulate values from `get(i)`

```java
int sum = 0;
for (int i = 0; i < values.size(); i++)
{
    sum += values.get(i);
}
double average = (double) sum / values.size();
```

Use `values.size()` for the divisor.

## Max

### Initialize from an existing element

```java
int max = values.get(0);
for (int i = 1; i < values.size(); i++)
{
    if (values.get(i) > max)
    {
        max = values.get(i);
    }
}
```

This works for negative numbers too.

## Count a Property

### Selection inside traversal

```java
int oddCount = 0;
for (int value : values)
{
    if (value % 2 != 0)
    {
        oddCount++;
    }
}
```

This is a frequency counter for an `ArrayList`.

## Duplicates

### Compare each pair once

```java
for (int i = 0; i < list.size(); i++)
{
    for (int j = i + 1; j < list.size(); j++)
    {
        if (list.get(i).equals(list.get(j)))
        {
            return true;
        }
    }
}
return false;
```

Use `equals` for objects such as strings.

## Rotate Right

### Remove from one end and add to the other

```java
String last = list.remove(list.size() - 1);
list.add(0, last);
```

For a new list version, add elements into a separate `ArrayList` instead of modifying the original.

## Reverse into a New List

### Build in opposite order

```java
ArrayList<String> reversed = new ArrayList<String>();
for (String item : list)
{
    reversed.add(0, item);
}
```

Each new item is inserted at the front.

## Parallel Data Structures

### Same index connects related lists

```java
ArrayList<String> names = new ArrayList<String>();
ArrayList<Integer> scores = new ArrayList<Integer>();

System.out.println(names.get(i) + ": " + scores.get(i));
```

Parallel lists require careful index consistency. A class is often a cleaner design.

## Classroom Check

### A strong answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- remove safely by traversing backward when needed
- compute sum, average, min, and max with `ArrayList` methods
- count elements that match a property
- use nested loops to detect duplicates
- rotate or reverse a list while explaining how indices change
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
